title: The Columbus Memo
briefing: A three-page memo with your initials leaked overnight, privately calling the $52 billion Ohio chip-plant subsidies you championed in Columbus 'a jobs mirage with good lighting.' Reporters want confirmation it's real before the six o'clock broadcast. You can confirm it and reframe, or call it a forgery and dare them to prove otherwise.

choices.label: Confirm it and explain the thinking
choices.result: You own the memo, walk reporters through the subsidy math line by line, and reframe it as candor. Columbus grumbles for a day, then the story moves on.

news.headline: President confirms Ohio memo, defends internal debate as healthy
At least this White House admits what the subsidies cost
Memo confirms the gap between the podium and the policy
POTUS says YEAH I WROTE THAT 😤 Ohio, deal with it

social.text: admitting it is annoyingly the smart move here. columbus respects candor.
wish the stump speech matched the memo but ok, honesty counts

choices.advisor: If it's real, confirm it before someone else defines it for you.

choices.label: Call it a fabrication
choices.result: You flatly deny it. Two days later the original surfaces with metadata from your own comms office, and now the lie is the story instead of the memo.

news.headline: BUSTED: White House denied a memo it wrote itself
Metadata contradicts presidential denial of Ohio memo
Denying real documents only feeds the wolves
LIAR LIAR: the MEMO was REAL the WHOLE TIME 🔥📄

social.text: the metadata doesn't lie. the president apparently does.

choices.advisor: Never deny a document you can't un-write. The forensics always win.

title: The Secretary's Portfolio
briefing: Energy Secretary Dana Whitfield still holds an $18 million stake in Meridian Grid Systems, which just won a $2.1 billion federal transmission contract. The optics are a gift to your opponents, and she insists it's all technically legal. You can force her to divest or step aside, or let her ride out the headlines.

choices.label: Order full divestment or resignation
choices.result: You give Whitfield 48 hours to divest or walk. She sells the Meridian stake at a loss, the ethics office signs off, and the story loses its hook by Friday.

news.headline: Energy Secretary Whitfield divests Meridian stake after conflict questions
Forced divestment is the bare minimum, but they did it
President acts fast to clear an ethics cloud
SELL SELL SELL: Whitfield dumps $18M in stock to keep her CHAIR 💺

social.text: divest or leave. that's the rule. glad someone enforced it.

choices.advisor: A clean secretary is cheaper than a month of Meridian headlines.

choices.label: Defend her and call it a witch hunt
choices.result: You circle the wagons and dismiss the story as partisan noise. Then Meridian lands a second contract — $600 million for grid sensors — and the whole cabinet's holdings go under the microscope.

news.headline: President shields Whitfield as second Meridian contract emerges
Conflict-of-interest questions widen from Energy to full cabinet
Loyalty is admirable; this is reckless
SHE WON A SECOND CONTRACT?! cabinet stock check NOW 📈🚨

social.text: calling accountability a witch hunt is the oldest play in the book

choices.advisor: Defending one conflict invites a hunt for every other one. Don't.

title: Delgado Under Oath
briefing: Your deputy chief of staff, Ray Delgado, told the Senate Judiciary Committee he'd had no contact with a surveillance contractor — and visitor logs show four meetings. The transcript is public, and Senator Carol Braddock of Maine is circling the word 'perjury.' You can cut him loose and let the process run, or vouch for him and gamble your own credibility on his.

choices.label: Accept his resignation, cooperate fully
choices.result: You accept Delgado's resignation, pledge full cooperation, and refer the matter to Justice. Painful loss of a loyalist, but the institution holds and so do you.

news.headline: Delgado resigns amid false-testimony probe; White House cooperates
Lying to Congress should cost a job. It did
President chooses the rule of law over a friend
DELGADO GONE: you don't FIB to the SENATE and stay 👋

social.text: he lied under oath and he's out. system worked for once.

choices.advisor: Loyalty to a person ends where loyalty to the office begins.

choices.label: Publicly vouch for him
choices.result: You stake your name on Delgado's honesty. A fifth visitor log drops within the week, and Braddock's committee pivots from his credibility to yours.

news.headline: President stakes credibility on aide who misled Judiciary panel
New visitor log deepens false-testimony case against Delgado
Tying yourself to a perjury risk is a rookie error
POTUS PINKY-SWEARS the liar isn't lying 🤞💀

social.text: now it's the president's word vs the visitor logs. terrible bet.

choices.advisor: Don't lend your credibility to someone who already spent theirs.

title: The Map Room Dinners
briefing: A Beacon reporter has receipts: fourteen donors who gave $500,000 apiece got private Map Room dinners and edits on a draft methane rule. It looks exactly like access for sale. You can publish the meeting logs and tighten the rules, or insulate the donors and tough out the coverage.

choices.label: Release the logs, ban donor policy meetings
choices.result: You post every meeting log and announce a firewall between fundraising and policy. Critics grumble it should've existed already, but the access story collapses by the weekend.

news.headline: White House publishes donor logs, bars Map Room policy dinners
Transparency on big money? We'll take the win
A firewall on access cools a damaging story
EVERY donor dinner DUMPED online 🍽️📜 receipts for DAYS

social.text: publishing the logs is how you kill an access story. nice.

choices.advisor: Disclosure turns a scandal into a rule. Cheaper than a denial.

choices.label: Refuse to release the logs
choices.result: You claim the logs are private and protect the donors. A West Wing steward leaks the guest list anyway — two hedge fund founders, a casino magnate — and now the cover-up is the story.

news.headline: STONEWALL: White House hides who bought the Map Room dinners
Leaked guest list undercuts White House secrecy claims
Hiding the donor list only confirms the worst read
WHAT'S ON THE LIST?! POTUS guards the GUEST BOOK 👀🔒

social.text: if it were clean they'd show us. it's never clean.

choices.advisor: A guarded guest list always leaks. Then it's secrecy plus the dinners.

title: Denny's Jakarta Deal
briefing: Your brother Denny used the family name to land a $200 million port-dredging contract in Jakarta, and his Indonesian partner is now telling investors he 'has a line to the President.' Nothing illegal on your end, but the smell carries from the Potomac to the Java Sea. You can publicly distance yourself and refer it for review, or quietly protect family and hope it stays small.

choices.label: Distance yourself, refer for review
choices.result: You state plainly that Denny speaks for no one but himself and ask ethics counsel to review any overlap. It's a tense Thanksgiving, but the line holds.

news.headline: President disavows brother's Jakarta venture, orders review
Drawing a hard line on a relative? Surprisingly right
Family is family, but the office comes first here
POTUS THROWS BRO UNDER THE BUS 🚌 'he speaks for himself' 😬

social.text: told his own brother to pound sand. that's the standard.

choices.advisor: Blood doesn't get a security clearance. Put daylight between you now.

choices.label: Quietly shield your brother
choices.result: You stay silent and lean on people to drop it. The partner, construction magnate Suryo Hartanto, repeats the 'line to the President' pitch at a Singapore investor summit, and the deal becomes an international embarrassment.

news.headline: President silent as brother trades on his name in Jakarta
Indonesian partner repeats claim of direct line to the President
Protecting family at the nation's expense is indefensible
'A LINE TO THE PREZ' 📞🌏 the JAKARTA bro deal won't DIE

social.text: every day of silence is free advertising for hartanto. yikes.

choices.advisor: Silence reads as endorsement. In Jakarta it reads as policy.

title: The Vanishing Emails
briefing: A National Archives officer flags that five weeks of staff emails covering the $9 billion border-surveillance contract award were 'auto-deleted' under a policy nobody remembers approving. It might be sloppiness, it might be worse. You can order a forensic recovery and report it, or let the gap stand and call it routine retention.

choices.label: Order recovery and disclose the gap
choices.result: You bring in forensic teams, recover 94 percent of the messages, and disclose the lapse alongside the fix. Embarrassing on process, but it kills any cover-up narrative.

news.headline: White House recovers deleted contract emails, discloses lapse
Recovering the record beats shredding it
Owning a process failure defuses a ticking bomb
FROM THE DEAD: deleted emails RESURRECTED 💀📧 spooky

social.text: they recovered them instead of memory-holing it. correct call.

choices.advisor: A recovered record is a story for a day. A missing one haunts you for months.

choices.label: Call it routine and let the gap stand
choices.result: You shrug it off as standard retention. A full backup turns up on a contractor's server in Reston, Virginia, proving the deletion targeted exactly the contract-award week.

news.headline: COVER-UP: Reston backup proves emails weren't routinely deleted
Contractor backup contradicts 'routine retention' claim
A convenient deletion is the reddest of flags
THE BACKUP EXISTS 😱💾 'routine' my FOOT 🚩🚩

social.text: there is ALWAYS a backup in northern virginia. they should know this.

choices.advisor: Nothing is ever truly deleted. Pretending otherwise is the trap.

title: The Whisper Campaign
briefing: The National Meteor, a supermarket tabloid, is shopping an affair rumor about you — thin on proof, heavy on insinuation — and your team is split on whether to engage. Ignore it and it might fade, or it might fester. You can address it once and briefly, or go to war with the outlet.

choices.label: Address it once, plainly, then move on
choices.result: You give one calm, flat denial in the briefing room and refuse a circus. With nothing new to chase, the Meteor's story deflates within a news cycle.

news.headline: President issues brief denial of tabloid rumor
A short, firm answer starves a smear
Rumor lacked proof; president didn't take the bait
POTUS shuts it down COLD 🥶 'next question' energy

social.text: one clean denial and back to work. that's how you handle it.

choices.advisor: Answer once, briefly, then never again. Engagement is oxygen.

choices.label: Launch an aggressive counter-attack
choices.result: You stand up a war room, threaten a $100 million libel suit, and attack the Meteor by name from the podium. The escalation makes a thin rumor look like a story worth a week of coverage.

news.headline: President's furious response elevates a flimsy tabloid rumor
Legal threats keep Meteor story in the headlines
Overreacting handed the smear a second life
WHY SO MAD?? 😏 the LADY doth protest TOO MUCH 🔥

social.text: screaming about it is how a nothing rumor trends for a week

choices.advisor: The angrier you get, the more real the rumor looks. Don't feed it.

title: A Pardon For Sale?
briefing: K Street lobbyist Gerald Vance is reportedly telling clients that a $2 million 'legal defense fund' donation could grease a presidential pardon for Leonard Marsh, a financier doing eight years for a $340 million Ponzi scheme. You never authorized any such thing, but the whisper is spreading and Marsh's name is already on the pardon list. You can refer it to a special counsel and freeze the list, or wave it off as gossip and proceed.

choices.label: Refer to special counsel, freeze the list
choices.result: You freeze the pardon list and hand the Vance matter to an independent prosecutor before lunch. The decisiveness signals you have nothing to hide and quarantines the rot.

news.headline: President refers pardon-for-cash claim to special counsel
Freezing the list was the only honest move
Self-referral signals confidence, not guilt
POTUS SICS the SPECIAL COUNSEL on his OWN pardon list 😳⚖️

social.text: freezing the list and calling in a prosecutor. that's how you stay clean.

choices.advisor: A pardon-for-cash whisper is radioactive. Quarantine it publicly, now.

choices.label: Dismiss it and proceed with the pardon
choices.result: You call it gossip and sign the Marsh pardon anyway. Vance's voicemails surface within days, the timing looks damning, and 'pardon for sale' becomes a defining headline.

news.headline: PARDON FOR SALE: president signs as Vance tapes emerge
Voicemails raise questions about timing of Marsh pardon
Signing through the whisper was indefensible
CASH FOR CLEMENCY 💸🔓 the TAPES are OUT and it's UGLY

social.text: signed the marsh pardon while the voicemails existed?? unbelievable.

choices.advisor: Of every scandal, this is the one that ends presidencies. Do not proceed.

title: Boxes In The Garage
briefing: A staffer found eleven cartons of classified documents — some marked SECRET//NOFORN, covering NATO force posture — in the garage of your lake house outside Traverse City, Michigan. Nobody's accused you of anything yet, but the National Archives and the press will both want answers. You can self-report and invite a full search, or sit on it and hope a quiet return goes unnoticed.

choices.label: Self-report and invite a full search
choices.result: You notify the Archives immediately, hand back all eleven cartons, and consent to an FBI sweep of the property. It's an embarrassing headline for a day, but cooperation is the whole story.

news.headline: President self-reports classified documents, invites FBI search
Self-reporting beats getting caught, full stop
Cooperation turns a mishap into a footnote
CLASSIFIED in the GARAGE?? 📦🔐 at least POTUS FESSED UP

social.text: called it in himself and opened the doors. that's the move.

choices.advisor: Mishandling is a mistake. Hiding it is a crime. Self-report today.

choices.label: Quietly return them and say nothing
choices.result: You arrange a discreet return through a former aide and tell no one. A courier logs the Traverse City handoff, the log leaks, and routine carelessness starts to look like concealment.

news.headline: Leaked log: classified docs returned in secret, never disclosed
Undisclosed Michigan document return raises concealment questions
A quiet return was the worst available option
SECRET HANDOFF EXPOSED 🤫📦 why HIDE it?! 🚨

social.text: every handoff gets logged. trying to hide it just adds a crime.

choices.advisor: A quiet return becomes a loud cover-up the instant anyone logs it.
